question:
Suppose that a finite group has exactly $n$ elements of order $p$,
where $p$ is a prime. Prove that either $n=0$ or $p$ divides $n+1$.
solution:
In all solutions, let $G$ be a finite group of order $m$.

\textbf{First solution:}
By Lagrange's theorem, if $m$ is
not divisible by $p$, then $n = 0$. Otherwise, let $S$ be the set of
$p$-tuples $(a_0,\dots,a_{p-1}) \in G^p$ such that $a_0 \cdots a_{p-1} = e$;
then $S$ has cardinality $m^{p-1}$, which is divisible by $p$.
Note that this set is invariant under cyclic permutation, that is,
if $(a_0,\dots,a_{p-1}) \in S$, then $(a_1,\dots,a_{p-1},a_0) \in S$ also.
The fixed points under this operation are the tuples
$(a,\dots,a)$ with $a^p = e$; all other tuples can be grouped into orbits
under cyclic permutation, each of which has size $p$. Consequently,
the number of $a \in G$ with $a^p = e$ is divisible by $p$; since that number
is $n+1$ (only $e$ has order 1), this proves the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Anand Deopurkar)
Assume that $n > 0$, and let $H$ be any subgroup of $G$ of order $p$.
Let $S$ be the set of all elements of $G \setminus H$
of order dividing $p$, and let
$H$ act on $G$ by conjugation. Each orbit has size $p$ except for those
which consist of individual elements $g$ which commute with $H$.
For each such $g$, $g$ and $H$ generate an elementary abelian subgroup of
$G$ of order $p^2$. However, we can group these $g$ into sets of size
$p^2-p$ based on which subgroup they generate together with $H$.
Hence the cardinality of $S$ is divisible by $p$; adding the $p-1$
nontrivial elements of $H$ gives $n \equiv -1 \pmod{p}$ as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Let $S$ be the set of elements in $G$ having order dividing $p$, and let
$H$ be an elementary abelian $p$-group of maximal order in $G$.  If
$|H|=1$, then we are done.  So assume $|H|=p^k$ for some $k\geq 1$, and
let $H$ act on $S$ by conjugation.  Let $T\subset S$ denote the set of
fixed points of this action.  Then the size of every $H$-orbit on $S$
divides $p^k$, and so $|S|\equiv |T| \pmod{p}$.
On the other hand, $H\subset T$, and if $T$ contained an element not in $H$,
then that would contradict the maximality of $H$.  It follows that
$H=T$, and so $|S|\equiv |T| = |H| = p^k \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$,
i.e., $|S|=n+1$ is a multiple of $p$.

\textbf{Remark:} This result is a theorem of Cauchy; the first solution
above is due to McKay. A more general (and more difficult) result was proved
by Frobenius: for any positive integer $m$, if $G$ is a finite group of order
divisible by $m$, then the number of elements of $G$ of order dividing $m$
is a multiple of $m$.

descriptive_long.question: Suppose that a finite group has exactly $ordercount$ elements of order $primeorder$, where $primeorder$ is a prime. Prove that either $ordercount=0$ or $primeorder$ divides $ordercount+1$.
descriptive_long.solution:
In all solutions, let $finitegroup$ be a finite group of order $grouporder$.

\textbf{First solution:}
By Lagrange's theorem, if $grouporder$ is
not divisible by $primeorder$, then $ordercount = 0$. Otherwise, let $orderset$ be the set of
$primeorder$-tuples $(tuplezero,\dots,tuplelast) \in finitegroup^{primeorder}$ such that $tuplezero \cdots tuplelast = e$;
then $orderset$ has cardinality $grouporder^{primeorder-1}$, which is divisible by $primeorder$.
Note that this set is invariant under cyclic permutation, that is,
if $(tuplezero,\dots,tuplelast) \in orderset$, then $(tupleone,\dots,tuplelast,tuplezero) \in orderset$ also.
The fixed points under this operation are the tuples
$(tupleelem,\dots,tupleelem)$ with $tupleelem^{primeorder} = e$; all other tuples can be grouped into orbits
under cyclic permutation, each of which has size $primeorder$. Consequently,
the number of $tupleelem \in finitegroup$ with $tupleelem^{primeorder} = e$ is divisible by $primeorder$; since that number
is $ordercount+1$ (only $e$ has order 1), this proves the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Anand Deopurkar)
Assume that $ordercount > 0$, and let $psubgroup$ be any subgroup of $finitegroup$ of order $primeorder$.
Let $orderset$ be the set of all elements of $finitegroup \setminus psubgroup$
of order dividing $primeorder$, and let
$psubgroup$ act on $finitegroup$ by conjugation. Each orbit has size $primeorder$ except for those
which consist of individual elements $groupelem$ which commute with $psubgroup$.
For each such $groupelem$, $groupelem$ and $psubgroup$ generate an elementary abelian subgroup of
$finitegroup$ of order $primeorder^2$. However, we can group these $groupelem$ into sets of size
$primeorder^2-primeorder$ based on which subgroup they generate together with $psubgroup$.
Hence the cardinality of $orderset$ is divisible by $primeorder$; adding the $primeorder-1$
nontrivial elements of $psubgroup$ gives $ordercount \equiv -1 \pmod{primeorder}$ as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Let $orderset$ be the set of elements in $finitegroup$ having order dividing $primeorder$, and let
$psubgroup$ be an elementary abelian $primeorder$-group of maximal order in $finitegroup$.  If
$|psubgroup|=1$, then we are done.  So assume $|psubgroup|=primeorder^{rankpower}$ for some $rankpower\geq 1$, and
let $psubgroup$ act on $orderset$ by conjugation.  Let $fixedset\subset orderset$ denote the set of
fixed points of this action.  Then the size of every $psubgroup$-orbit on $orderset$
divides $primeorder^{rankpower}$, and so $|orderset|\equiv |fixedset| \pmod{primeorder}$.
On the other hand, $psubgroup\subset fixedset$, and if $fixedset$ contained an element not in $psubgroup$,
then that would contradict the maximality of $psubgroup$.  It follows that
$psubgroup=fixedset$, and so $|orderset|\equiv |fixedset| = |psubgroup| = primeorder^{rankpower} \equiv 0 \pmod{primeorder}$,
i.e., $|orderset|=ordercount+1$ is a multiple of $primeorder$.

\textbf{Remark:} This result is a theorem of Cauchy; the first solution
above is due to McKay. A more general (and more difficult) result was proved
by Frobenius: for any positive integer $grouporder$, if $finitegroup$ is a finite group of order
divisible by $grouporder$, then the number of elements of $finitegroup$ of order dividing $grouporder$
is a multiple of $grouporder$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Suppose that a finite group has exactly $wavelength$ elements of order $raincloud$,
where $raincloud$ is a prime. Prove that either $wavelength=0$ or $raincloud$ divides $wavelength+1$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
In all solutions, let $labyrinth$ be a finite group of order $undertone$.

\textbf{First solution:}
By Lagrange's theorem, if $undertone$ is
not divisible by $raincloud$, then $wavelength = 0$. Otherwise, let $turnpike$ be the set of
$raincloud$-tuples $(driftwoodzero,\dots,driftwoodlast) \in labyrinth^{raincloud}$ such that $driftwoodzero \cdots driftwoodlast = e$;
then $turnpike$ has cardinality $undertone^{raincloud-1}$, which is divisible by $raincloud$.
Note that this set is invariant under cyclic permutation, that is,
if $(driftwoodzero,\dots,driftwoodlast) \in turnpike$, then $(driftwoodone,\dots,driftwoodlast,driftwoodzero) \in turnpike$ also.
The fixed points under this operation are the tuples
$(driftwood,\dots,driftwood)$ with $driftwood^{raincloud} = e$; all other tuples can be grouped into orbits
under cyclic permutation, each of which has size $raincloud$. Consequently,
the number of $driftwood \in labyrinth$ with $driftwood^{raincloud} = e$ is divisible by $raincloud$; since that number
is $wavelength+1$ (only $e$ has order 1), this proves the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Anand Deopurkar)
Assume that $wavelength > 0$, and let $horsepower$ be any subgroup of $labyrinth$ of order $raincloud$.
Let $turnpike$ be the set of all elements of $labyrinth \setminus horsepower$
of order dividing $raincloud$, and let
$horsepower$ act on $labyrinth$ by conjugation. Each orbit has size $raincloud$ except for those
which consist of individual elements $stoneware$ which commute with $horsepower$.
For each such $stoneware$, $stoneware$ and $horsepower$ generate an elementary abelian subgroup of
$labyrinth$ of order $raincloud^{2}$. However, we can group these $stoneware$ into sets of size
$raincloud^{2}-raincloud$ based on which subgroup they generate together with $horsepower$.
Hence the cardinality of $turnpike$ is divisible by $raincloud$; adding the $raincloud-1$
nontrivial elements of $horsepower$ gives $wavelength \equiv -1 \pmod{raincloud}$ as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Let $turnpike$ be the set of elements in $labyrinth$ having order dividing $raincloud$, and let
$horsepower$ be an elementary abelian $raincloud$-group of maximal order in $labyrinth$.  If
$|horsepower|=1$, then we are done.  So assume $|horsepower|=raincloud^{slingback}$ for some $slingback\geq 1$, and
let $horsepower$ act on $turnpike$ by conjugation.  Let $timeshift\subset turnpike$ denote the set of
fixed points of this action.  Then the size of every $horsepower$-orbit on $turnpike$
divides $raincloud^{slingback}$, and so $|turnpike|\equiv |timeshift| \pmod{raincloud}$.
On the other hand, $horsepower\subset timeshift$, and if $timeshift$ contained an element not in $horsepower$,
then that would contradict the maximality of $horsepower$.  It follows that
$horsepower=timeshift$, and so $|turnpike|\equiv |timeshift| = |horsepower| = raincloud^{slingback} \equiv 0 \pmod{raincloud}$,
i.e., $|turnpike|=wavelength+1$ is a multiple of $raincloud$.

\textbf{Remark:} This result is a theorem of Cauchy; the first solution
above is due to McKay. A more general (and more difficult) result was proved
by Frobenius: for any positive integer $undertone$, if $labyrinth$ is a finite group of order
divisible by $undertone$, then the number of elements of $labyrinth$ of order dividing $undertone$
is a multiple of $undertone$.}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Suppose that a finite group has exactly $uncounted$ elements of order $composite$,
where $composite$ is a prime. Prove that either $uncounted=0$ or $composite$ divides $uncounted+1$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
In all solutions, let $chaosset$ be a finite group of order $fraction$.

\textbf{First solution:}
By Lagrange's theorem, if $fraction$ is
not divisible by $composite$, then $uncounted = 0$. Otherwise, let $emptiness$ be the set of
$composite$-tuples $(ultimate,\dots,beginner) \in chaosset^{composite}$ such that $ultimate \cdots beginner = opposite$;
then $emptiness$ has cardinality $fraction^{composite-1}$, which is divisible by $composite$.
Note that this set is invariant under cyclic permutation, that is,
if $(ultimate,\dots,beginner) \in emptiness$, then $(terminal,\dots,beginner,ultimate) \in emptiness$ also.
The fixed points under this operation are the tuples
$(absence,\dots,absence)$ with $absence^{composite} = opposite$; all other tuples can be grouped into orbits
under cyclic permutation, each of which has size $composite$. Consequently,
the number of $absence \in chaosset$ with $absence^{composite} = opposite$ is divisible by $composite$; since that number
is $uncounted+1$ (only $opposite$ has order 1), this proves the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Anand Deopurkar)
Assume that $uncounted > 0$, and let $outsider$ be any subgroup of $chaosset$ of order $composite$.
Let $emptiness$ be the set of all elements of $chaosset \setminus outsider$
of order dividing $composite$, and let
$outsider$ act on $chaosset$ by conjugation. Each orbit has size $composite$ except for those
which consist of individual elements $aggregate$ which commute with $outsider$.
For each such $aggregate$, $aggregate$ and $outsider$ generate an elementary abelian subgroup of
$chaosset$ of order $composite^2$. However, we can group these $aggregate$ into sets of size
$composite^2-composite$ based on which subgroup they generate together with $outsider$.
Hence the cardinality of $emptiness$ is divisible by $composite$; adding the $composite-1$
nontrivial elements of $outsider$ gives $uncounted \equiv -1 \pmod{composite}$ as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Let $emptiness$ be the set of elements in $chaosset$ having order dividing $composite$, and let
$outsider$ be an elementary abelian $composite$-group of maximal order in $chaosset$.  If
$|outsider|=1$, then we are done.  So assume $|outsider|=composite^{rootless}$ for some $rootless\geq 1$, and
let $outsider$ act on $emptiness$ by conjugation.  Let $wanderers\subset emptiness$ denote the set of
fixed points of this action.  Then the size of every $outsider$-orbit on $emptiness$
divides $composite^{rootless}$, and so $|emptiness|\equiv |wanderers| \pmod{composite}$.
On the other hand, $outsider\subset wanderers$, and if $wanderers$ contained an element not in $outsider$,
then that would contradict the maximality of $outsider$.  It follows that
$outsider=wanderers$, and so $|emptiness|\equiv |wanderers| = |outsider| = composite^{rootless} \equiv 0 \pmod{composite}$,
i.e., $|emptiness|=uncounted+1$ is a multiple of $composite$.

\textbf{Remark:} This result is a theorem of Cauchy; the first solution
above is due to McKay. A more general (and more difficult) result was proved
by Frobenius: for any positive integer $fraction$, if $chaosset$ is a finite group of order
divisible by $fraction$, then the number of elements of $chaosset$ of order dividing $fraction$
is a multiple of $fraction$.}

garbled_string.question:
Suppose that a finite group has exactly $hpqlsvzx$ elements of order $xdrmquab$,
where $xdrmquab$ is a prime. Prove that either $hpqlsvzx=0$ or $xdrmquab$ divides $hpqlsvzx+1$.
garbled_string.solution:
In all solutions, let $jzxqvtrm$ be a finite group of order $flzvqust$.

\textbf{First solution:}
By Lagrange's theorem, if $flzvqust$ is
not divisible by $xdrmquab$, then $hpqlsvzx = 0$. Otherwise, let $vnglksad$ be the set of
$xdrmquab$-tuples $(yagldvop,\dots,ksrpvoae) \in jzxqvtrm^{xdrmquab}$ such that $yagldvop \cdots ksrpvoae = e$;
then $vnglksad$ has cardinality $flzvqust^{xdrmquab-1}$, which is divisible by $xdrmquab$.
Note that this set is invariant under cyclic permutation, that is,
if $(yagldvop,\dots,ksrpvoae) \in vnglksad$, then $(nqpwlsae,\dots,ksrpvoae,yagldvop) \in vnglksad$ also.
The fixed points under this operation are the tuples
$(yvzrnkie,\dots,yvzrnkie)$ with $yvzrnkie^{xdrmquab} = e$; all other tuples can be grouped into orbits
under cyclic permutation, each of which has size $xdrmquab$. Consequently,
the number of $yvzrnkie \in jzxqvtrm$ with $yvzrnkie^{xdrmquab} = e$ is divisible by $xdrmquab$; since that number
is $hpqlsvzx+1$ (only $e$ has order 1), this proves the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Anand Deopurkar)
Assume that $hpqlsvzx > 0$, and let $pyhwmnqe$ be any subgroup of $jzxqvtrm$ of order $xdrmquab$.
Let $vnglksad$ be the set of all elements of $jzxqvtrm \setminus pyhwmnqe$
of order dividing $xdrmquab$, and let
$pyhwmnqe$ act on $jzxqvtrm$ by conjugation. Each orbit has size $xdrmquab$ except for those
which consist of individual elements $tqwmslpa$ which commute with $pyhwmnqe$.
For each such $tqwmslpa$, $tqwmslpa$ and $pyhwmnqe$ generate an elementary abelian subgroup of
$jzxqvtrm$ of order $xdrmquab^2$. However, we can group these $tqwmslpa$ into sets of size
$xdrmquab^2-xdrmquab$ based on which subgroup they generate together with $pyhwmnqe$.
Hence the cardinality of $vnglksad$ is divisible by $xdrmquab$; adding the $xdrmquab-1$
nontrivial elements of $pyhwmnqe$ gives $hpqlsvzx \equiv -1 \pmod{xdrmquab}$ as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Let $vnglksad$ be the set of elements in $jzxqvtrm$ having order dividing $xdrmquab$, and let
$pyhwmnqe$ be an elementary abelian $xdrmquab$-group of maximal order in $jzxqvtrm$.  If
$|pyhwmnqe|=1$, then we are done.  So assume $|pyhwmnqe|=xdrmquab^{pnahwiole}$ for some $pnahwiole\geq 1$, and
let $pyhwmnqe$ act on $vnglksad$ by conjugation.  Let $ldbxrsoc\subset vnglksad$ denote the set of
fixed points of this action.  Then the size of every $pyhwmnqe$-orbit on $vnglksad$
divides $xdrmquab^{pnahwiole}$, and so $|vnglksad|\equiv |ldbxrsoc| \pmod{xdrmquab}$.
On the other hand, $pyhwmnqe\subset ldbxrsoc$, and if $ldbxrsoc$ contained an element not in $pyhwmnqe$,
then that would contradict the maximality of $pyhwmnqe$.  It follows that
$pyhwmnqe=ldbxrsoc$, and so $|vnglksad|\equiv |ldbxrsoc| = |pyhwmnqe| = xdrmquab^{pnahwiole} \equiv 0 \pmod{xdrmquab}$,
i.e., $|vnglksad|=hpqlsvzx+1$ is a multiple of $xdrmquab$.

\textbf{Remark:} This result is a theorem of Cauchy; the first solution
above is due to McKay. A more general (and more difficult) result was proved
by Frobenius: for any positive integer $flzvqust$, if $jzxqvtrm$ is a finite group of order
divisible by $flzvqust$, then the number of elements of $jzxqvtrm$ of order dividing $flzvqust$
is a multiple of $flzvqust$.}

kernel_variant.question:
Let \ell  be a prime and r \geq  1 an integer.  
For a finite group G set  

 C_r(G)= { (g_1,\ldots ,g_r)\in G^r  |  g_i^\ell  = e for every i and g_i g_j = g_j g_i for all i,j }.  

Thus C_r(G) consists of the ordered r-tuples of pairwise-commuting \ell -elements of G.

A)  Assume that \ell  divides |G|.  Prove that |C_r(G)| is divisible by \ell .  
   (If \ell \nmid |G| then C_r(G)= {(e,\ldots ,e)}, so no divisibility can be expected.)

B)  Show that the exponent obtained in A) is best possible:  

 for every prime \ell  and every r \geq  1 there exists a finite group G with  
  \nu _\ell (|C_r(G)|)=1.

A single, uniform proof is expected; avoid a long case-by-case discussion.  
(Hints for B:  for \ell =2 one may take the symmetric group S_3; for odd \ell  one may take the group PSL_2(\ell ).)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let \ell  be a fixed prime, r \geq  1 an integer, and G a finite group.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Part A.  Divisibility of |C_r(G)| by \ell  (under \ell  | |G|)  
We argue by induction on r.

Base step r = 1.  
Put  

 T := {(x_0,\ldots ,x_{\ell -1}) \in  G^{\ell }  |  x_0x_1\cdot \cdot \cdot x_{\ell -1}=e}.  

Clearly |T| = |G|^{\ell -1}, which is divisible by \ell  because \ell  | |G|.  
Let the cyclic group C_\ell  = \langle \sigma \rangle  act on T by the left shift  
 \sigma \cdot (x_0,\ldots ,x_{\ell -1}) = (x_1,\ldots ,x_{\ell -1},x_0).  
All orbits have size \ell  except the fixed points, which satisfy x_0 = \cdot \cdot \cdot  = x_{\ell -1} and hence are precisely the diagonal tuples (x,\ldots ,x) with x^\ell  = e.  The class equation gives  

 |T| \equiv  # {x\in G | x^\ell =e}  (mod \ell ).  

Because |T| is a multiple of \ell , the set S_1(G):= {x\in G | x^\ell  = e} has cardinality divisible by \ell .  Now  

 |C_1(G)| = |S_1(G)| = \ell \cdot k     for some k\in \mathbb{N},

so |C_1(G)| is a multiple of \ell , establishing the base case.

Induction step.  Assume r \geq  2 and that the statement holds for r-1.  
For h=(h_1,\ldots ,h_{r-1})\in C_{r-1}(G) put H:=\langle h_1,\ldots ,h_{r-1}\rangle ; this is an elementary abelian \ell -subgroup, hence H \leq  C_G(H).  Define  

 S_h := { g\in C_G(H) | g^\ell =e }.        (1)

Apply the already-proved base case r = 1 to the finite group C_G(H): because \ell  divides |C_G(H)| (H is non-trivial unless h=(e,\ldots ,e), but the argument below also works for the trivial H), the set S_h has size divisible by \ell .  Next observe that an r-tuple in C_r(G) is obtained by adjoining to h any element of S_h as its last coordinate, and every such choice is admissible.  Hence the map  

 C_{r-1}(G) \times  S_h  \to   C_r(G),    (h,g) \mapsto  (h_1,\ldots ,h_{r-1},g)

is bijective inside each fibre over h, yielding  

 |C_r(G)| = \sum _{h\in C_{r-1}(G)} |S_h|.        (2)

By the induction hypothesis each summand |S_h| is divisible by \ell , therefore their sum (2) is divisible by \ell .  This completes the induction and proves part A. \blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------------
Part B.  Sharpness of the exponent \nu _\ell (|C_r(G)|) = 1  

We produce, for every prime \ell  and every r \geq  1, a finite group G with exactly one factor \ell  in |C_r(G)|.

(i)  The trivial case r = 1.  
Take G = C_\ell .  Then C_1(G)=G and |C_1(G)|=\ell , so \nu _\ell (|C_1(G)|)=1.

(ii)  A uniform family for every r \geq  2.  
If \ell  = 2 we take the symmetric group S_3; for odd \ell  we take the simple group  

 G = PSL_2(\ell )   (of order \ell (\ell ^2-1)/2).

Standard facts we use (see, e.g., Huppert-Blackburn or any text on PSL_2(q)):

* Every non-identity \ell -element of PSL_2(\ell ) lies in a unique cyclic subgroup of order \ell , and its centraliser is precisely that subgroup.  
* The number c of cyclic subgroups of order \ell  in PSL_2(\ell ) equals \ell +1 (the number of points of the projective line over F_\ell ).

Exactly the same two facts hold for \ell  = 2 inside S_3 (c = 3).

Counting commuting r-tuples.  
Two non-trivial \ell -elements commute iff they belong to the same cyclic subgroup of order \ell .  Consequently every member of C_r(G) is either the all-identity tuple E = (e,\ldots ,e) or else lies entirely inside a unique cyclic subgroup of order \ell .  Inside one such subgroup H \cong  C_\ell  there are \ell ^r ordered r-tuples of \ell -elements, of which precisely one is E.  Summing over the c subgroups we obtain  

 |C_r(G)| = 1 + c(\ell ^r - 1).             (3)

Substituting c = \ell +1 gives  

 |C_r(G)| = 1 + (\ell +1)(\ell ^r - 1)  
     = (\ell +1)\ell ^r - \ell   
     = \ell  \cdot  [(\ell +1)\ell ^{\,r-1} - 1].     (4)

Because the bracket is congruent to -1 modulo \ell , it is not divisible by \ell , so \nu _\ell (|C_r(G)|)=1.  This proves the sharpness for every r \geq  2.

Combining (i) and (ii) we have produced, for every prime \ell  and every r \geq  1, a finite group G with \nu _\ell (|C_r(G)|)=1, completing part B. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-dimensional setting.  Instead of counting single ℓ-elements (r=1), the problem asks for the number of ordered r-tuples of pairwise-commuting ℓ-elements, dramatically enlarging the search space and the combinatorial complexity.

2. Additional group-theoretic structure.  The proof must control centralisers of arbitrary tuples and repeatedly invoke Cauchy’s theorem inside those centralisers; simple orbit-counting no longer suffices.

3. Multi-layer induction.  Each new coordinate introduces a fresh layer of centraliser analysis and a fresh application of Cauchy, so the argument grows in depth with r.  The original problem needs one counting trick; the enhanced variant needs r nested applications.

4. Interacting concepts.  The solution blends three ideas: (i) centraliser containment, (ii) recursive extension of homomorphisms, and (iii) divisibility inherited from subgroups.  Any shortcut or naive counting overlooks the dependence of later coordinates on all previous ones.

5. Stronger conclusion.  While the original theorem supplies a single factor of ℓ, the enhanced result forces ℓ^r—a power that increases with the dimension—making it strictly stronger for every r > 1 and unattainable by the original arguments alone.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let \ell  be a prime and r \geq  1 an integer.  
For a finite group G set  

 C_r(G)= { (g_1,\ldots ,g_r)\in G^r  |  g_i^\ell  = e for every i and g_i g_j = g_j g_i for all i,j }.  

Thus C_r(G) consists of the ordered r-tuples of pairwise-commuting \ell -elements of G.

A)  Assume that \ell  divides |G|.  Prove that |C_r(G)| is divisible by \ell .  
   (If \ell \nmid |G| then C_r(G)= {(e,\ldots ,e)}, so no divisibility can be expected.)

B)  Show that the exponent obtained in A) is best possible:  

 for every prime \ell  and every r \geq  1 there exists a finite group G with  
  \nu _\ell (|C_r(G)|)=1.

A single, uniform proof is expected; avoid a long case-by-case discussion.  
(Hints for B:  for \ell =2 one may take the symmetric group S_3; for odd \ell  one may take the group PSL_2(\ell ).)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let \ell  be a fixed prime, r \geq  1 an integer, and G a finite group.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Part A.  Divisibility of |C_r(G)| by \ell  (under \ell  | |G|)  
We argue by induction on r.

Base step r = 1.  
Put  

 T := {(x_0,\ldots ,x_{\ell -1}) \in  G^{\ell }  |  x_0x_1\cdot \cdot \cdot x_{\ell -1}=e}.  

Clearly |T| = |G|^{\ell -1}, which is divisible by \ell  because \ell  | |G|.  
Let the cyclic group C_\ell  = \langle \sigma \rangle  act on T by the left shift  
 \sigma \cdot (x_0,\ldots ,x_{\ell -1}) = (x_1,\ldots ,x_{\ell -1},x_0).  
All orbits have size \ell  except the fixed points, which satisfy x_0 = \cdot \cdot \cdot  = x_{\ell -1} and hence are precisely the diagonal tuples (x,\ldots ,x) with x^\ell  = e.  The class equation gives  

 |T| \equiv  # {x\in G | x^\ell =e}  (mod \ell ).  

Because |T| is a multiple of \ell , the set S_1(G):= {x\in G | x^\ell  = e} has cardinality divisible by \ell .  Now  

 |C_1(G)| = |S_1(G)| = \ell \cdot k     for some k\in \mathbb{N},

so |C_1(G)| is a multiple of \ell , establishing the base case.

Induction step.  Assume r \geq  2 and that the statement holds for r-1.  
For h=(h_1,\ldots ,h_{r-1})\in C_{r-1}(G) put H:=\langle h_1,\ldots ,h_{r-1}\rangle ; this is an elementary abelian \ell -subgroup, hence H \leq  C_G(H).  Define  

 S_h := { g\in C_G(H) | g^\ell =e }.        (1)

Apply the already-proved base case r = 1 to the finite group C_G(H): because \ell  divides |C_G(H)| (H is non-trivial unless h=(e,\ldots ,e), but the argument below also works for the trivial H), the set S_h has size divisible by \ell .  Next observe that an r-tuple in C_r(G) is obtained by adjoining to h any element of S_h as its last coordinate, and every such choice is admissible.  Hence the map  

 C_{r-1}(G) \times  S_h  \to   C_r(G),    (h,g) \mapsto  (h_1,\ldots ,h_{r-1},g)

is bijective inside each fibre over h, yielding  

 |C_r(G)| = \sum _{h\in C_{r-1}(G)} |S_h|.        (2)

By the induction hypothesis each summand |S_h| is divisible by \ell , therefore their sum (2) is divisible by \ell .  This completes the induction and proves part A. \blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------------
Part B.  Sharpness of the exponent \nu _\ell (|C_r(G)|) = 1  

We produce, for every prime \ell  and every r \geq  1, a finite group G with exactly one factor \ell  in |C_r(G)|.

(i)  The trivial case r = 1.  
Take G = C_\ell .  Then C_1(G)=G and |C_1(G)|=\ell , so \nu _\ell (|C_1(G)|)=1.

(ii)  A uniform family for every r \geq  2.  
If \ell  = 2 we take the symmetric group S_3; for odd \ell  we take the simple group  

 G = PSL_2(\ell )   (of order \ell (\ell ^2-1)/2).

Standard facts we use (see, e.g., Huppert-Blackburn or any text on PSL_2(q)):

* Every non-identity \ell -element of PSL_2(\ell ) lies in a unique cyclic subgroup of order \ell , and its centraliser is precisely that subgroup.  
* The number c of cyclic subgroups of order \ell  in PSL_2(\ell ) equals \ell +1 (the number of points of the projective line over F_\ell ).

Exactly the same two facts hold for \ell  = 2 inside S_3 (c = 3).

Counting commuting r-tuples.  
Two non-trivial \ell -elements commute iff they belong to the same cyclic subgroup of order \ell .  Consequently every member of C_r(G) is either the all-identity tuple E = (e,\ldots ,e) or else lies entirely inside a unique cyclic subgroup of order \ell .  Inside one such subgroup H \cong  C_\ell  there are \ell ^r ordered r-tuples of \ell -elements, of which precisely one is E.  Summing over the c subgroups we obtain  

 |C_r(G)| = 1 + c(\ell ^r - 1).             (3)

Substituting c = \ell +1 gives  

 |C_r(G)| = 1 + (\ell +1)(\ell ^r - 1)  
     = (\ell +1)\ell ^r - \ell   
     = \ell  \cdot  [(\ell +1)\ell ^{\,r-1} - 1].     (4)

Because the bracket is congruent to -1 modulo \ell , it is not divisible by \ell , so \nu _\ell (|C_r(G)|)=1.  This proves the sharpness for every r \geq  2.

Combining (i) and (ii) we have produced, for every prime \ell  and every r \geq  1, a finite group G with \nu _\ell (|C_r(G)|)=1, completing part B. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-dimensional setting.  Instead of counting single ℓ-elements (r=1), the problem asks for the number of ordered r-tuples of pairwise-commuting ℓ-elements, dramatically enlarging the search space and the combinatorial complexity.

2. Additional group-theoretic structure.  The proof must control centralisers of arbitrary tuples and repeatedly invoke Cauchy’s theorem inside those centralisers; simple orbit-counting no longer suffices.

3. Multi-layer induction.  Each new coordinate introduces a fresh layer of centraliser analysis and a fresh application of Cauchy, so the argument grows in depth with r.  The original problem needs one counting trick; the enhanced variant needs r nested applications.

4. Interacting concepts.  The solution blends three ideas: (i) centraliser containment, (ii) recursive extension of homomorphisms, and (iii) divisibility inherited from subgroups.  Any shortcut or naive counting overlooks the dependence of later coordinates on all previous ones.

5. Stronger conclusion.  While the original theorem supplies a single factor of ℓ, the enhanced result forces ℓ^r—a power that increases with the dimension—making it strictly stronger for every r > 1 and unattainable by the original arguments alone.
